title: Splayed Leg X Bench
summary: A small bench with legs that splay out on a gentle 5° double bevel and an X-brace base. A compound-miter exercise in a one-board build.
description:
This little bench looks casually mid-century, but the splay is doing precise work: the legs kick out at a 5° double bevel, which is what gives it that planted, stable stance instead of looking like a box on sticks. That double bevel is the whole skill here — it's the same cut as a simple stool leg, and a compound miter saw set to 5° makes it repeatable.

The honest warning from the source builder is real: once you've cut four legs with a 5° double bevel, it becomes genuinely confusing which end and which face is which. Mark the inside corners of every leg the moment you cut it, or you'll assemble a bench that leans the wrong way.

The aprons are also cut at 5° (ends not parallel) so they meet the splayed legs square. Everything joins with pocket holes or countersunk screws and glue. The top overhangs about the same on each side; mark the underside 1-1/2" in from the ends to locate it before you screw it down.

tools.note: A compound miter saw set to 5° is what makes the double-bevel legs repeatable.
Countersink bit if you're not using pocket holes.

cutList.note: Ends parallel, cut at a 5° angle off square AND a 5° bevel (double bevel).
Both ends 5° off square, not parallel, short point to short point.
Both ends 5° off square, not parallel.
Both ends 5° off square, not parallel.

steps.title: Cut the legs at a double bevel
steps.body: Cut the four legs at a 5° double bevel — the saw angled 5° off square and tilted 5° for the bevel. As soon as each leg is cut, mark its inside corner. With a 5° double bevel it's genuinely hard to tell which end and face is which once you start assembling, and mismarked legs splay the wrong way.

steps.title: Build the end assemblies
steps.body: Mark the placement of the end aprons and predrill (pocket holes or countersunk screws and glue). Center the aprons on the legs as shown. Build both ends.

steps.title: Add the side aprons
steps.body: Attach the side aprons to the two end assemblies. They're cut at 5° off square with ends not parallel; the measurement given is short point to short point. This ties the base into a rigid frame.

steps.title: Add the cross supports
steps.body: Add the cross stretchers between the aprons. Doing this as its own step means you can lengthen the bench later by simply changing these — and it keeps the base square if you nudge the aprons in or out.

steps.title: Attach the top and finish
steps.body: Mark the underside of the top 1-1/2" in from both ends to locate it, giving even overhangs. Attach with screws and glue (screw through the top if you're painting; from underneath if the top will show). Fill, sand, and finish.

images.alt: A small bench with outward-splayed legs and an X-brace base
